channel.title: DFIR Radar
channel.description: Keeping DFIR Intelligence on your Radar. The best DFIR research from hundreds of sources, filtered for relevance.

item.title: “Your shipment has arrived” email hides remote access software
item.description:
New DHL phishing campaign delivers preconfigured SimpleHelp RMM tool via PDF lure. PDF contains blurred image with fake Microsoft button that downloads .scr installer from compromised Vietnamese 🇻🇳 logistics domain longhungphatlogistics[.]vn.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2045050432234696959

item.title: What’s New in the BloodHound Query Library: BYOL, OpenGraph, Multi-Server, and More
item.description:
BloodHound Query Library adds 34 new AD/Azure queries, multi-server support, and BYOL feature to import custom query sources. Includes PurpleKnight mapping coverage and built-in Cypher cheat sheet for AD enumeration.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044974737936310671

item.title: CVE-2026-3055 & CVE-2026-4368: Inside the NetScaler "CitrixBleed 3" Memory Overread
item.description:
CVE-2026-3055 (CVSS 9.3) allows unauthenticated memory overread in Citrix NetScaler SAML IdPs, exposing session tokens and credentials via NSC_TASS cookies. Active exploitation confirmed since March 27. Patch immediately to 14.1-66.59+ or 13.1-62.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044960141582204961

item.title: Lumma Stealer infection with Sectop RAT (ArechClient2), (Fri, Apr 17th)
item.description:
SANS ISC researcher documents Lumma Stealer infection chain leading to Sectop RAT deployment. Attack uses fake Adobe Premiere Pro 2026 crack distributed through password-protected archives with 806MB inflated executables.
Technical breakdown: • Initial delivery via fake software crack sites impersonating MEGA cloud storage • Lumma Stealer packed in password-protected 7zip (password: 6919) with null-byte padding evasion • SHA256: c7489e3bf546c5f2d958ac833cc7dbca4368dfba03a792849bc99c48a6b2a14f (archive), 4849f76dafbef516df91fecfc23a72afffaf77ade51f805eae5ad552bed88923 (inflated EXE) • 9 Lumma C2 domains identified: cankgmr[.]cyou, carytui[.]vu, decrnoj[.]club, genugsq[.]best
Follow-up payload: • Sectop RAT (ArechClient2) deployed as NetGui.dll via rundll32 LoadForm export • C2 traffic to 91.92.241[.]102:9000 and :443 with custom encryption • Persistence established on infected Windows hosts
Hunt for oversized executables (>100MB) with high entropy ratios and rundll32 spawning network connections to non-standard ports. Full IOCs and sandbox analysis links available in SANS report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044944672754217026

item.title: Obsidian Plugin Abuse Delivers PHANTOMPULSE RAT in Targeted Finance, Crypto Attacks
item.description:
Novel campaign abuses Obsidian note-taking app to deliver PHANTOMPULSE RAT targeting finance and crypto sectors. Elastic Security Labs tracks this as REF6598 cluster.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044929399980491040

item.title: UAC-0247 Attack Detection: AGINGFLY Malware Targets Hospitals, Local Governments, and FPV Operators in Ukraine
item.description:
UAC-0247 deploys AGINGFLY malware against Ukrainian 🇺🇦 hospitals and local governments via humanitarian-themed phishing. Campaign uses LNK→HTA→shellcode injection chain, abusing mshta.exe and legitimate processes for persistence.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044914403431133263

item.title: Hackers exploit Marimo flaw to deploy NKAbuse malware from Hugging Face
item.description:
CVE-2026-39987 in Marimo Python notebook actively exploited to deploy NKAbuse malware via typosquatted Hugging Face Spaces. Attackers pivot to credential theft and lateral movement within hours of disclosure. Upgrade to v0.23.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044899163268059521

item.title: Void Stealer: The Infostealer Malware Quietly Targeting Organizations in 2026
item.description:
Void Stealer infostealer bypasses EDR via syscall-level evasion and uses Steam profiles for C2 resolution, allowing infrastructure rotation without disrupting campaigns. IOCs and MITRE ATT&CK mappings available in the full report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044884191943221251

item.title: APK Malformation Found in Thousands of Android Malware Samples
item.description:
Android malware families including Teabot, TrickMo, Godfather deploy APK malformation to crash static analysis tools while preserving functionality. Cleafy releases open-source Malfixer tool to repair corrupted APKs for analysis.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044869045606887907

item.title: Into The Rainbow: Google’s NTLMv1 Rainbow Tables Explained in a Bit Too Much Detail
item.description:
SpecterOps breaks down Google's 8.8TB NTLMv1 rainbow tables that enable offline NT hash recovery from legacy authentication responses. New tooling makes this attack accessible to more practitioners.
Technical breakdown: • 4,096 rainbow tables (2.147GB each) cover 99.9% of DES key space using precompute-lookup-check methodology • Tables target static challenge 1122334455667788, splitting NT hash into three 7-byte DES keys for recovery • Recovery process: precompute generates endpoints, lookup finds candidates in tables, check verifies actual ciphertext presence • Works with DumpGuard to obtain NTLMv1 responses from Credential Guard-enabled systems • Performance: ~1 hour per ciphertext on RTX 3080 Ti + NVMe drives (12min precompute, 45min lookup, 12min check)
New open-source tooling includes gpu_lookup, precompute, candidate_lookup, and candidate_check applications. Anonymous lookup possible by separating precompute/check phases from table lookup.
Hunt for NTLMv1 authentication events in network traffic and Windows logs - this dramatically lowers the barrier for NT hash recovery from legacy protocols.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044854071228543017

item.title: Attackers Actively Exploiting Critical Vulnerability in Ninja Forms – File Upload Plugin
item.description:
Critical file upload flaw in Ninja Forms WordPress plugin enables unauthenticated RCE. Mass exploitation began same day as disclosure with 118,600+ blocked attempts across 50,000 installations.
Key technical details: • CVE-2026-0740, CVSS 9.8 - affects Ninja Forms File Upload ≤3.3.26, patched in 3.3.27 • Missing destination filename validation in NF_FU_AJAX_Controllers_Uploads::handle_upload function • Attackers upload disguised PHP webshells (PDF/JPG headers) + .htaccess files via path traversal • Exploitation via POST to /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=nf_fu_upload with malicious multipart form data
Attack methodology: • Valid file headers (PDF-1.4, GIF89a) bypass source validation while storing as .php extensions • Path traversal (../../) places shells in webroot outside upload directories • Webshells use php_uname(), shell_exec(), system() for reconnaissance and command execution • .htaccess modification forces .txt files to execute as PHP for steganography
DFIR artifacts: • Check /wp-content/uploads/ and webroot for suspicious .php files with recent timestamps • Review access logs for nf_fu_upload action from IPs: 124[.]248[.]183[.]139, 152[.]42[.]221[.]239, 124[.]108[.]54[.]86 • Hunt multipart POST requests with mismatched Content-Type headers and destination filenames
Hunt for recent PHP files in WordPress directories with minimal file sizes (<5KB) containing php_uname() or shell_exec() functions.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044838973504123277

item.title: Payouts King Takes Aim at the Ransomware Throne
item.description:
Payouts King ransomware emerges from BlackBasta's ashes as former affiliates pivot to new operation. Analysis reveals sophisticated evasion techniques including direct syscalls, custom obfuscation algorithms, and selective file encryption targeting high-value data.
Key technical details: • Uses 4,096-bit RSA + 256-bit AES-CTR encryption with custom CRC32 algorithm (poly: 0xBDC65592) for string obfuscation • Implements direct syscalls to bypass EDR hooks when terminating 131+ AV/EDR processes (ZwTerminateProcess, ZwOpenProcess) • Persistence via Mozilla-themed scheduled tasks: \Mozilla\UpdateTask and \Mozilla\ElevateTask (T1053.005) • Partial encryption strategy: files >10MB split into 13 blocks with 50% encrypted per block for performance • Anti-sandbox evasion requires -i parameter with valid CRC checksum to execute encryption
Attack methodology: • Initial access via spam bombing + vishing calls impersonating IT staff (T1566.001) • Victims tricked into Microsoft Teams calls and Quick Assist remote access (T1219) • Drops .ZWIAAW extension on encrypted files, readme_locker.txt ransom note • Clears shadow copies, event logs, and recycle bin for anti-forensics (T1070)
Hunt for schtasks.exe creating Mozilla-named tasks, SetFileInformationByHandle API calls for file renaming, and processes making direct syscalls to ntdll without standard API wrappers.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044823891101515794

item.title: Dissecting Sapphire Sleet’s macOS intrusion from lure to compromise
item.description:
Microsoft Threat Intelligence exposes North Korean 🇰🇵 Sapphire Sleet's sophisticated macOS campaign targeting crypto users through fake Zoom updates. Attack bypasses all native macOS protections via social engineering and user-initiated execution.
Technical breakdown: • Initial access: Malicious .scpt file ("Zoom SDK Update.scpt") with hidden AppleScript payload below thousands of blank lines • Execution chain: Cascading curl-to-osascript commands with campaign tracking user-agents (mac-cur1 through mac-cur5) • TCC bypass: Manipulates ~/Library/Application Support/com.apple.TCC/TCC.db via Finder to grant AppleEvents permissions silently • Credential theft: Fake systemupdate.app presents authentic macOS password dialog, validates via dscl, exfiltrates to Telegram Bot API • Persistence: Multiple backdoors (com.apple.cli, services/icloudz, com.google.chromes.updaters) with launch daemon masquerading as legitimate services
Data exfiltration targets: • Cryptocurrency wallets (Ledger, Exodus, browser extensions for Sui, Phantom, TronLink, Coinbase, OKX) • Browser credentials, cookies, keychains across Chrome/Brave/Arc • Telegram Desktop sessions enabling account takeover • SSH keys, shell history, Apple Notes for lateral movement
Hunt for unsigned binaries in Library directories, monitor TCC database modifications, and block .scpt execution from untrusted sources. Full IOCs and hunting queries provided.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044808896146014454

item.title: Android Bankers: 4 Campaigns In A Row
item.description:
Zimperium exposes four sophisticated Android banking trojan campaigns actively targeting 800+ financial, crypto, and social media apps with advanced multi-stage infection chains and near-zero detection rates.
Key technical details: • RecruitRat, SaferRat, Astrinox, and Massiv families use Session Installation API abuse to bypass Android sideloading restrictions (T1626.001) • Multi-stage droppers hide payloads in res/assets directories, using DexClassLoader for runtime DEX loading • APK tampering at ZIP level with unsupported compression methods crashes standard analysis tools (APKTool, JADX) • Overlay attacks harvest device PINs and inject HTML phishing pages for 700+ targeted apps • Advanced evasion: AES/GCM encrypted payloads, RC4-encrypted C2 traffic, root/AV detection checks
Attack methodology: • Initial access via phishing sites mimicking job platforms, streaming services, and app stores • Persistence through Accessibility Services abuse, invisible app icons, and anti-delete mechanisms • Real-time screen capture via MediaProjection API provides "virtual seat" access to attackers • Dynamic overlay injection synchronized with legitimate app launches intercepts credentials and 2FA tokens
DFIR artifacts: • Monitor Session Installation API calls from non-system processes • Hunt for apps requesting Accessibility Services + SYSTEM_ALERT_WINDOW permissions • Detect WebView overlay activities with suspicious HTML injection patterns
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044793645463933236

item.title: ClickFix Phishing Campaign Masquerading as a Claude Installer
item.description:
New ClickFix campaign targets EU/US organizations using fake Claude AI installer, delivering multi-stage PowerShell payload through sophisticated obfuscation layers. Rapid7 MDR detected initial compromise via Windows Run utility monitoring, preventing full infection chain.
Key technical details: • Initial vector: mshta execution via `download-version[.]1-5-8[.]com/claude.msixbundle` • Masquerades as MSIX bundle but contains embedded HTA with VBS obfuscation • Multi-stage PowerShell chain: AMSI bypass → process injection → encrypted shellcode • Uses MD5 hash of COMPUTERNAME+USERNAME for C2 URL generation • Final payload: Information stealer targeting stored credentials
Attack methodology: • Leverages ClickFix social engineering to trick users into running malicious commands • Commands stored in RunMRU registry key (tracks last 26 Run utility executions) • PowerShell stages progressively deobfuscate using custom routines and XOR encryption • Process injection via .NET interop: NtAllocateVirtualMemory → NtCreateThreadEx
DFIR artifacts: • Monitor HKEY_CURRENT_USER\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Explorer\RunMRU • Look for mshta with suspicious URLs in process command lines • PowerShell execution with AMSI bypass attempts (amsiContext overwrite)
IOCs: claude.msixbundle (SHA256: 2b99ade9...), domains oakenfjrod[.]ru, download-version[.]1-5-8[.]com. Hunt for mshta processes with external URLs and review user clipboard history for ClickFix indicators.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044778534581715177

item.title: From clinics to government: UAC-0247 expands cyber campaign across Ukraine
item.description:
CERT-UA exposes UAC-0247's expanded campaign targeting Ukrainian 🇺🇦 clinics and government entities with multi-stage malware for data theft. Active March-April 2026 operation uses AI-generated phishing sites and deploys AGINGFLY, CHROMELEVATOR for browser credential theft.
Attack chain breakdown: • Initial access: Phishing emails with humanitarian aid lures leading to HTA execution chains • AGINGFLY C# malware uses AES-CBC encrypted WebSockets, downloads/compiles functions on-the-fly (T1140, T1027) • CHROMELEVATOR steals Chromium browser credentials, ZAPIXDESK targets WhatsApp data • SILENTLOOP PowerShell script manages C2 via Telegram with backup mechanisms • Persistence through scheduled tasks, process injection into RuntimeBroker.exe (T1053.005, T1055)
Lateral movement tools include RUSTSCAN for network scanning, LIGOLO-NG/CHISEL for covert tunneling. Modified WIREGUARD executable deployed XMRIG cryptocurrency miner in one incident.
Block execution of LNK, HTA, JS files and restrict mshta.exe, powershell.exe, wscript.exe to reduce attack surface. Hunt for RuntimeBroker.exe with network connections.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044763404213329957

item.title: BlobPhish: The Phantom Phishing Campaign Hiding in Browser Memory
item.description:
BlobPhish campaign uses browser blob objects to deliver phishing pages entirely in memory, evading traditional detection. Active since October 2024, targeting Microsoft 365 and major US 🇺🇸 financial institutions with sophisticated evasion techniques.
Key technical details: • JavaScript loader decodes Base64 payload, creates blob object with `window.URL.createObjectURL()`, forces navigation via hidden anchor, then destroys evidence • Targets Chase, Capital One, E*TRADE, Charles Schwab, American Express alongside Microsoft 365/OneDrive/SharePoint • Exfiltration endpoints follow patterns: `/res.php`, `/tele.php`, `/panel.php` with form-data POST requests • Loader variants: `blob.html`, `blom.html`, `bloji.html`, `emailandpasssss.html` hosted on compromised WordPress sites
Attack chain bypasses URL reputation, proxy logs, and cache-based detection since phishing page exists only as `blob:https://` scheme in browser memory. Campaign spans 18+ months with infrastructure rotation across compromised legitimate sites.
Hunt for POST requests to `*/res.php` or `*/tele.php` endpoints with form-data containing credentials, and monitor for `blob:https://` URLs in browser history. Full YARA rules and IOC list available in report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044748401846161769

item.title: PowMix botnet targets Czech workforce
item.description:
Cisco Talos uncovers PowMix botnet targeting Czech 🇨🇿 workforce since December 2025. Uses AMSI bypass, randomized C2 beaconing, and mimics legitimate REST API calls to evade network detection.
Technical breakdown: • Initial access via phishing ZIP containing malicious LNK file executing PowerShell loader • AMSI bypass through reflection technique targeting AmsiUtils.amsiInitFailed field • Persistence via scheduled task with randomized hex names (e.g., "289c2e236761") executing daily at 11:00 AM • C2 communication uses jittered intervals (0-261s, then 1075-1450s) with encrypted heartbeat data in URL paths • Bot ID generation via CRC32 checksum of Windows ProductID + config hash
Attack artifacts: • ZIP marker delimiter "zAswKoK" for payload extraction • Registry query: HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion (ProductID) • Mutex creation: "Global\[BotID]" prevents multiple instances • XOR keys: HpSWSb, qDQyxQE, bKUxmhyAe, HymzqLse, KsEYwmgSF, ujCPOEPU • Commands: (self-delete), (C2 migration)
Hunt for scheduled tasks with hex-only names executing explorer.exe with LNK arguments, and PowerShell processes with AMSI bypass indicators. Full IOCs and detection rules available.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044733320651559042

item.title: A fake Slack download is giving attackers a hidden desktop on your machine
item.description:
Fake Slack installer from typosquatting domain deploys invisible HVNC backdoor, creating hidden desktop session for financial fraud operations. Legitimate Slack installation runs as decoy while loader injects into explorer.exe.
Technical breakdown: • Dropper `slack-4-49-81.exe` writes two files: `slack.tmp` (legitimate Squirrel installer) and `svc.tmp` (519KB loader with randomized PE sections) • Loader contacts C2 at `94[.]232[.]46[.]16:8081`, downloads encrypted HVNC payload to shared memory • Section-based injection into explorer.exe using NtCreateSection/NtCreateThreadEx APIs (T1055.011) • HVNC creates invisible desktop session for covert browser access and account manipulation • Anti-analysis via debugger detection, dynamic API resolution, minimal import table
Forensic artifacts: • Registry persistence: `HKCU\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run\com.squirrel.slack.slack` (matches legitimate Slack) • Temp files: `loader_log.txt`, `wmiprvse_*.tmp` pattern for payload storage • Process injection into explorer.exe detectable via ETW or memory analysis
Hunt for unsigned executables with randomized section names making network connections during Slack installation timeframe.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044718057847656466

item.title: CVE-2026-34486: Apache Tomcat Tribes Regression Creates Unauthenticated RCE Path
item.description:
CVE-2026-34486 in Apache Tomcat Tribes creates unauthenticated RCE via regression in EncryptInterceptor - decryption failures now fail open, allowing Java deserialization bypass. Affects 9.0.116, 10.1.53, 11.0.20.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044672711474004367

item.title: From RAM to revelation: how Windows manages memory and how Volatility reads it
item.description:
Deep dive into Windows memory management fundamentals reveals how kernel structures enable memory forensics and why Volatility can reconstruct system state from RAM dumps.
Technical architecture breakdown: • Virtual memory uses multi-level page tables with CR3 register storing page directory base, enabling per-process address space isolation • Page Table Entries exist in multiple forensic states: valid (in RAM), transition (evicted but recoverable), demand-zero, pagefile, and prototype • VAD (Virtual Address Descriptor) trees track memory regions - private executable memory without file backing indicates code injection • Pool allocations tagged with 4-byte ASCII strings enable structure discovery via pool scanning (e.g., "Proc" for EPROCESS)
Modern Windows complications: • Memory compression in Windows 10+ stores evicted pages in MemCompression pseudo-process using undocumented SMKM_STORE structures • KASLR randomizes kernel addresses, requiring heuristic scanning for kernel debugger data block (KDBG) signatures • Structure layouts change between versions, requiring symbol table updates from Microsoft PDB files
Volatility 3 uses layered architecture with physical→translation→virtual memory mapping, enabling transparent address translation across multiple contexts.
Compare windows.pslist vs windows.psscan output to detect process hiding - any EPROCESS visible in pool scan but missing from active list indicates rootkit activity.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044657647966630273

item.title: Critical Nginx-ui MCP Flaw Actively Exploited in the Wild
item.description:
CVE-2026-33032 (CVSS 9.8) in nginx-ui allows unauthenticated attackers to fully compromise nginx servers via missing auth on /mcp_message endpoint. Over 2,600 public instances identified. Update to v2.3.4 immediately or disable MCP functionality.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044642612305404068

#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044627403478057245

item.description:

item.title: [Guest Diary] Compromised DVRs and Finding Them in the Wild, (Thu, Apr 16th)
item.description:
Massive botnet recruitment campaign targets 5,313 exposed Dahua DVRs globally with 2-second automated compromise chains. Research reveals 202+ confirmed compromised systems actively participating in malicious activity, with actual numbers likely far higher.
Key findings: • Attack chain uses default credentials (root/root) via Telnet, escalates to Unix shell in ~2 seconds (T1110.001, T1078, T1059.004) • Staging script tests file systems, creates hidden files in /dev/shm memory filesystem, verifies download capabilities (T1082, T1564.001, T1105) • Compromised Spanish 🇪🇸 DVR (46[.]6[.]14[.]135) launching attacks since November 2025, firmware unpatched since 2014 • PowerShell reconnaissance script cross-references Shodan hash searches with AbuseIPDB reports to identify active botnet members • 3.8% infection rate in sample suggests 200+ compromised devices globally, conservative estimate due to 90-day reporting window
Hunt for Telnet connections from DVR/IoT devices to internal networks, monitor for /dev/shm file creation, and baseline network utilities execution. Full PowerShell detection script available in the research.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044567047536054345

item.title: Using AI signals within malicious email for attack detection and threat hunting · Blog · Sublime Security
item.description:

item.title: CVE-2026-39987 update: How attackers weaponized marimo to deploy a blockchain botnet via HuggingFace
item.description:
Critical RCE in marimo Python notebook platform actively exploited 3 days post-disclosure. Attackers deploy novel NKAbuse variant via typosquatted HuggingFace Space, conduct credential harvesting and lateral movement to databases.
Technical details: • CVE-2026-39987: Pre-auth RCE in marimo, 9h41m disclosure-to-exploit gap • 11 unique IPs from 10 countries generated 662 exploit events (April 11-14) • Novel NKAbuse Go backdoor uses NKN blockchain for C2, hosted on vsccode-modetx[.]hf[.]space • Dropper SHA256: 25e4b2c4bb37f125b693a9c57b0e743eab2a3d98234f7519cd389e788252fd13
Attack chain: • Initial access via marimo WebSocket terminal exploitation • Credential extraction targeting AWS keys, DATABASE_URL, API tokens from env vars • Lateral movement to PostgreSQL (159[.]100[.]6[.]251) and Redis honeypots using leaked creds • Multiple reverse shell techniques: bash, Python, netcat across TCP/UDP ports
DFIR artifacts: • ~/.kagent/ directory and systemd user service kagent.service • Process name "kagent" (typosquats legitimate Kubernetes tool) • NKN protocol traffic patterns for blockchain-based C2 • Database enumeration queries in PostgreSQL/Redis logs
Hunt for kagent processes, ~/.kagent persistence, and monitor fd redirection to network sockets. Runtime detection catches reverse shells at syscall level regardless of obfuscation.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044461777937960976

item.title: Signed Adware Operation Disables Antivirus Across 23,000 Hosts
item.description:
Signed adware operation from UAE 🇦🇪 company Dragon Boss Solutions LLC silently disabled antivirus on 23,000+ hosts worldwide using legitimate certificates and update infrastructure. Campaign active since late 2024 with global reach across critical sectors.
Key technical details: • ClockRemoval.ps1 PowerShell script runs with SYSTEM privileges targeting Malwarebytes, Kaspersky, McAfee, ESET • Advanced Installer framework polls remote servers for MSI updates, bypassing detection through legitimate signing • Creates 5 scheduled tasks + WMI event subscriptions for persistence (T1053.005, T1546.003) • Kills AV processes every 100ms for 20 seconds at boot, modifies hosts file to redirect update domains to 0[.]0[.]0[.]0 • Adds Defender exclusions for "DGoogle" and "EMicrosoft" staging directories
Sinkhole operation revealed 23,565 unique IPs across 124 countries, including 221 universities, 41 OT networks, 35 government entities. US 🇺🇸 led infections at 54%, followed by France 🇫🇷, Canada 🇨🇦, UK 🇬🇧, Germany 🇩🇪.
Critical risk: Unregistered update domain could enable arbitrary payload delivery to compromised hosts with disabled AV protection.
Hunt for ClockRemoval.ps1 execution, suspicious scheduled tasks with 30-minute intervals, and hosts file modifications redirecting security vendor domains.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044446572948115572

item.title: Slowburn: Looking through AMD Platform Configuration Blobs infrastructure
item.description:
Researcher discovers critical SMM callout vulnerability in AMD Platform Configuration Blocks, affecting massive surface of AMD Athlon, EPYC, and Ryzen processors with potential for persistent system compromise.
Deep technical analysis reveals SMM Callout vulnerability (CVE-2025-54502) in AMD's APCB infrastructure:
• AmdApcbSmmV3 SMM driver registers ACPI SMI handler (GUID 6D7F690C-AAFA-4036-AB61D230DC2EA675) enabling external communication with SMM context • Command 0xA1CB0010 triggers APCB::Core::PurgeTypeData service which calls LocateProtocol from EFI_BOOT_SERVICES table inside SMM - classic SMM callout violation • Vulnerability occurs in APCB::Memory::AllocatePool and FreePool functions that improperly call boot services while executing in SMM context • APCB (AMD Platform Configuration Block) stores critical hardware configurations in SPI ROM with groups for PSP, memory, north bridge, and token management • Attack requires crafting EFI_MM_COMMUNICATE_HEADER with handler GUID and priority level ≤3 to trigger vulnerable code path
Exploitation scenarios include arbitrary SMRAM read/write primitives or misconfigured TSEG settings. Hunt for unusual SMM communication patterns and validate SMM drivers don't invoke boot services within SMM context.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044431447449067993

item.title: Black Shrantac exposes industrial environments to stealth ransomware risk through LOTL, double extortion tactics
item.description:
Black Shrantac ransomware group weaponizes legitimate admin tools and CVE-2024-3400 for stealth attacks across industrial sectors. Active since September 2025, combining LOTL tactics with double extortion to evade detection.
Technical breakdown: • Initial access via CVE-2024-3400 (CVSS 10.0) in EOL Palo Alto PAN-OS 11.0.0 devices, plants trojanized GlobalProtect MSI in firewall update portal • Persistence through SimpleHelp remote access service, Net Monitor for Employees Agent, and new AD domain accounts with elevated privileges • Credential harvesting using native klist[.]exe for Kerberos ticket enumeration and pass-the-ticket attacks (T1558.003) • Lateral movement via RDP, PSExec, MightyViewer VNC, and SSHFS-Win mounted drives - all legitimate tools mimicking admin activity • Defense evasion by disabling Defender via PowerShell, using vendor uninstall utilities, clearing event logs, renaming encryptor binaries
Hunt for scheduled tasks created by non-SYSTEM accounts pointing to user directories, and monitor Event ID 4769/4624 for Kerberos anomalies across multiple hosts.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044417652861354223

item.title: MiningDropper – A Global Modular Android Malware Campaign Operating at Scale
item.description:
Cyble uncovers MiningDropper, a sophisticated Android malware framework combining crypto mining with multi-payload delivery. Over 1,500 samples detected with 50%+ showing minimal AV detection, targeting India 🇮🇳, Europe, and LATAM through trojanized apps.
Technical breakdown: • Multi-stage architecture: XOR-obfuscated native code → AES-encrypted DEX payloads → split APK reconstruction • Trojanizes open-source LumoLight app, uses anti-emulation checks and filename-derived AES keys • Delivers BTMOB RAT (full device control, keylogging, WebView injections) or infostealers via configuration-driven payload selection • Distribution via phishing sites impersonating RTO services, banks (ICICI, SBI, HDFC), and telecom providers
Key artifacts: • Native library: librequisitionerastomous.so with XOR string obfuscation • Asset files: x7bozjy2pg4ckfhn (first stage), jajmanpongids (installer ZIP) • MITRE: T1660 (Phishing), T1575 (Native API), T1406 (Obfuscation), T1633 (Sandbox Evasion)
Hunt for Android apps with suspicious asset files using filename-derived AES patterns and native libraries performing XOR string deobfuscation. Full IOC list available on Cyble's GitHub.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044401197403058332

item.title: From fake Proton VPN sites to gaming mods, this Windows infostealer is everywhere
item.description:

Technical breakdown: • Multi-stage infection: Self-injection or DLL hijacking into RegAsm.exe using MSI/Node.js wrappers • Targets 25+ crypto wallet folders/registry keys plus major browsers (Chrome, Edge, Opera, Firefox) • Uses AES-CBC encryption for C2 comms with Telegram dead drop backup at t[.]me/gerj_threuh • CMSTP UAC bypass technique creates scheduled tasks with elevated privileges (T1548.002) • Process hollowing via NtCreateUserProcess/NtUnmapViewOfSection APIs (T1055.012)
Distribution vectors include fake domains vpn-proton-setup[.]com and get-proton-vpn[.]com, plus legitimate platforms hosting malicious archives like "OhmGraphite-0.36.1.zip" and "Sidebar Diagnostics-3.6.5.zip".
Hunt for RegAsm.exe with network connections, unsigned DLLs (WindowsCodecs.dll, iviewers.dll), and scheduled tasks referencing LOCALAPPDATA hidden directories. Full IOC list includes file hashes and malicious URLs.

Campaign details: • Attackers leverage n8n's trusted webhook infrastructure at tti.app.n8n[.]cloud to bypass email security filters • Phishing emails masquerade as OneDrive shares, delivering CAPTCHA-protected pages that download modified Datto and ITarian RMM tools as backdoors • Device fingerprinting via invisible tracking pixels embedded in spam emails automatically triggers HTTP GET requests to n8n webhooks • Payloads include "DownloadedOneDriveDocument.exe" and "OneDrive_Document_Reader_pHFNwtka_installer.msi" protected by Armadillo packer
Technical artifacts: • PowerShell command chains configure RMM tools as scheduled tasks before self-deletion • Fake installer GUIs display progress bars that reset to 0% to simulate failed installations • Webhook URLs contain tracking parameters including victim email addresses for precise attribution
Monitor for unexpected outbound connections to n8n[.]cloud domains and investigate high-volume traffic to AI automation platforms from internal sources. Full IOC list available on Talos GitHub.

item.title: Why DFIR teams need to look beyond the MBR when analyzing modern wipers
item.description:
Modern disk wipers have evolved beyond simple MBR corruption to target GPT metadata, NTFS structures, and leverage signed drivers via BYOVD techniques. Understanding these progression stages is critical for DFIR triage and recovery planning.
Evolution breakdown: • Legacy: MBR-only corruption (WhisperGate) - leaves file data recoverable via TestDisk/carving • Advanced: GPT + NTFS $MFT targeting (HermeticWiper, CaddyWiper) using IOCTL_DISK_SET_DRIVE_LAYOUT_EX • Evasive: BYOVD approach loading legitimate signed drivers (EaseUS via \\.\EPMNTDRV\%u device path) • Simplified: File-level overwrites (BiBi, Hatef, Hamsa) with 4KB blocks for cross-platform speed
Iranian 🇮🇷 threat actors increasingly favor simpler file-level destruction over complex disk manipulation for operational speed across mixed Windows/Linux environments.
Monitor for \\.\PhysicalDrive0 access, unexpected driver loads, partition layout IOCTLs, and USN Journal corruption patterns. Each technique closes different recovery paths - scope the method early to determine realistic restoration options.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044340850478682475

item.title: Securing the Software Supply Chain: How SentinelOne’s AI EDR Autonomously Blocked the CPU-Z Watering Hole Cyber Attack
item.description:
Supply chain attack: CPUID's CPU-Z distribution compromised for 19 hours on April 9, delivering STX RAT through legitimate download infrastructure. Attackers compromised cpuid[.]com API to redirect downloads to malicious payloads bundled with signed binaries.
Key technical details: • Malicious CRYPTBASE.dll loaded via DLL search order hijacking, bypassing system DLL • Process chain: cpuz_x64.exe → powershell.exe → csc.exe → cvtres.exe (abnormal for CPU-Z) • STX RAT payload: reflective PE loader with XXTEA encryption, no disk artifacts • Triple persistence: registry Run key + 68-minute scheduled task + MSBuild .proj files in AppData\Local • C2: DNS-over-HTTPS to 1[.]1[.]1[.]1 resolving welcome[.]supp0v3[.]com, custom encrypted protocol
Attack infrastructure reused from March 2026 FileZilla campaign - same C2 domain supp0v3[.]com and IP 147[.]45[.]178[.]61. 150+ confirmed victims across IT sectors, likely initial access broker operation targeting privileged users.
Hunt for CRYPTBASE.dll outside C:\Windows\System32, monitor CPUID applications spawning PowerShell, and watch for DoH queries to 1[.]1[.]1[.]1/dns-query patterns.

item.title: Digital Forensics: Analyzing Crashed ArduPilot Drones
item.description:
New methodology: Extracting flight data and mission plans from crashed ArduPilot drones reveals complete attack timelines, intended targets, and operational patterns for threat attribution and battlefield forensics.
Key technical details: • Two critical artifacts: .bin files (DataFlash logs with GPS, telemetry, timestamps) and .plan files (JSON waypoint missions showing intended routes) • Extract .bin logs from APM/LOGS/ directory on microSD card; recover .plan files via Mission Planner USB connection • Use mavlogdump.py to parse .bin files for takeoff coordinates, crash locations, speed profiles, and flight duration • QGroundControl visualizes .plan files showing spline waypoints, altitudes, hold patterns, and target areas
Analysis capabilities: • Cross-reference planned vs actual flight paths to identify deviations or mission failures • Extract precise crash coordinates, impact speed, and altitude from telemetry data • Calculate total flight time using takeoff/hit timestamps and TimeUS microsecond fields • Identify vehicle firmware, platform type, and configuration details
Methodology applies beyond aerial drones - ArduPilot runs on boats, submarines, ground rovers, making this valuable for diverse incident scenarios.
Parse recovered drone storage with mavlogdump.py and correlate .plan intentions against .bin reality for complete operational reconstruction.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044129539383799855

Key technical advances: • Agent targets A11-A18 SoCs (iPhone 8 through iPhone 16) using exploit chains for privilege escalation and sandbox escape • Covers remaining iOS 16.7-16.7.15 builds plus full iOS 17/18 branches for comprehensive keychain and filesystem access • A11 devices (iPhone 8/X) require agent method due to SEP patch blocking checkm8 if passcode ever configured • M-series iPads remain capped at iPadOS 16.6.1 pending future update
Certificate validation now requires internet connection for developer accounts created after June 6, 2021 🇺🇸 due to Apple's 🇺🇸 Provisioning Profile Queue requirements. iOS 17.3+ with Stolen Device Protection needs biometric auth for device trust.
Enhanced checkm8 support adds iOS 15.8.7, iPadOS 16.7.15, and iPadOS 18.7.5 coverage.
Route certificate validation through strict firewall allowing only PPQ traffic. Enable Developer Mode post-install on iOS 16+.

item.title: Digital Forensics: Extracting Secrets After a Cold Boot Attack
item.description:
New guide details cold boot attack methodology for RAM extraction from encrypted systems. Even locked machines with full disk encryption remain vulnerable to memory dumping via bootable USB drives on legacy BIOS systems.
Key technical details: • Attack exploits RAM data persistence after power loss - electrical charges fade gradually, not instantly • Uses bootable USB with GRUB4DOS + scraper64_haltonly.bin to dump physical memory to partition • Memory dump captured at /dev/sdb2, convertible via dd to ram.img for analysis • Volatility/Rekall frameworks reconstruct OS state: processes, registry hives, credentials, network connections
Attack methodology: • Force cold reboot via power cycling, hardware reset, or deliberate BSOD crash • Boot from USB bypasses OS memory clearing routines during normal shutdown • Scraper overwrites only ~820KB of RAM on systems with tens of GB - minimal forensic impact • Process takes time proportional to installed memory capacity
DFIR artifacts available: • Password hashes extractable from registry hives in memory • Domain credentials sometimes present in plaintext • Open files, clipboard contents, desktop screenshots may persist in graphical buffers • Full process listing and network connection state reconstruction possible
Important limitation: Modern EFI firmware writes random bytes across entire RAM during hardware initialization, effectively neutralizing this attack vector.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044023600420712743

item.title: Common Entra ID Security Assessment Findings – Part 4: Weak Conditional Access Policies
item.description:
Compass Security reveals critical gaps in Entra ID Conditional Access deployments that leave organizations vulnerable despite having policies in place. Common misconfigurations create exploitable bypasses for attackers with compromised credentials.
Key vulnerabilities identified: • Incomplete user targeting: MFA policies scoped to groups often miss users, leaving accounts unprotected. Role-based targeting fails with scoped assignments (Administrative Units not included in role conditions) • Resource targeting gaps: "Microsoft Admin Portals" protection doesn't cover Graph API access. Attackers can bypass portal restrictions using ROPC flow with first-party OAuth clients like Modern Workplace Customer API Native • Missing security info registration policies: Attackers with passwords can register their own MFA factors before general MFA policies activate, effectively bypassing multi-factor requirements • Built-in bypasses: Microsoft Intune Company Portal client (9ba1a5c7-f17a-4de9-a1f1-6178c8d51223) can bypass compliant device requirements while maintaining Graph API access with Device.Read.All permissions
Detection opportunities: • Monitor for Graph API tokens issued outside admin portals (check client_app_id in sign-in logs) • Alert on MFA registration from unusual locations/devices • Track ROPC authentication flows in audit logs • Review Conditional Access policy evaluation results for unexpected bypasses
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2044008523760115799

Fake Claude AI website distributes PlugX RAT via DLL sideloading attack. Campaign exploits AI tool popularity to deliver espionage malware through trojanized installer masquerading as Anthropic's chatbot service.
Technical details: • ZIP contains MSI installer dropping VBScript that launches legitimate Claude while copying NOVUpdate.exe, avk.dll, and encrypted .dat file to Startup folder • DLL sideloading (T1574.002) abuses legitimate G DATA antivirus updater to load malicious avk.dll • C2 communication to 8[.]217[.]190[.]58 over HTTPS within 22 seconds, hosted on Alibaba Cloud infrastructure • Self-deletion mechanism removes VBScript and batch file post-execution, leaving only sideloaded files and running process • Registry modification targeting TCP/IP settings to alter network behavior
Detection opportunities: • Monitor for NOVUpdate.exe with network connections from non-G DATA installations • Hunt for unsigned avk.dll files in Startup folders paired with legitimate signed executables • Alert on VBScript execution followed by rapid file deletion and HTTPS connections to cloud infrastructure
IOCs and detection signatures available in the Malwarebytes report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2043993436894708121

Attack progression: • Mass exploitation began March 20, 2026 (disclosure day) with 312K+ blocked attempts • Peak activity April 4-10 coinciding with free Wordfence protection rollout • Top attacking IPs: 209[.]146[.]60[.]26 (152K attempts), 49[.]156[.]40[.]126 (50K attempts) • Post-compromise: attackers edit functions.php to inject persistent malware
Hunt for POST requests to admin-ajax.php containing "kaliforms_form_process" action with suspicious entryCounter values. Review admin login logs for unusual authentication around March 20-April 10 timeframe.

item.title: JanelaRAT: a financial threat targeting users in Latin America
item.description:
JanelaRAT version 33 emerges as an evolved banking Trojan specifically targeting Brazilian 🇧🇷 and Mexican 🇲🇽 financial institutions with advanced session hijacking capabilities and daily C2 rotation.
Key technical details: • MSI dropper sideloads legitimate nevasca.exe + malicious PixelPaint.dll (JanelaRAT payload) • Protected with Eazfuscator obfuscation, uses AES encryption with MD5-derived keys • Dynamic C2 construction: concatenates obfuscated string + current date + DDNS suffix on port 443 • Targets 25+ Brazilian banks via hardcoded window title detection with 12-second delay • Full-screen overlay system mimics banking interfaces to harvest MFA tokens and credentials
Attack methodology: • Initial vector: phishing emails with fake invoice PDFs leading to malicious website downloads • Persistence via LNK shortcut in Startup folder pointing to renamed legitimate executable • User activity tracking with 10-minute inactivity monitoring for optimal remote operation timing • Real-time banking session manipulation including keystroke injection, mouse simulation, screenshot capture
DFIR artifacts: Monitor for nevasca.exe with network connections to DDNS services, LNK files in Startup folders, and PowerShell execution with PixelPaint.dll references. Hunt for processes checking banking software presence.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2043963182885024005

item.title: JitterDropper
item.description:
OALabs identifies JitterDropper, a Rust-compiled Windows dropper with distinctive API jitter patterns that acts as a fingerprint across nine builds spanning five weeks of active development.
Technical breakdown: • Two variants: Variant I embeds payload in .rdata with multi-pass decryption, Variant II downloads 122-byte shellcode from pixeldrain[.]com • Distinctive sleep jitter bounds per API: 60ms before InternetOpenA, 150ms before StringFromGUID2, 100ms before CoGetObject, 120ms before CoUninitialize • Anti-analysis features include CheckRemoteDebuggerPresent/IsDebuggerPresent pair, GUI cover window with no message pump, EnumWindows stall loop (21-27 iterations) • GetTickCount elapsed-check gates defeat sandboxes that compress time or skip Sleep calls - exits if delta exceeds threshold (e.g., 1153ms) • Delivers unknown .NET stealer and Vidar payloads via sRDI/Donut reflective loader
The 32-byte Lemire mod 1000 reduction pattern from rustc is compiler-generated, but the per-API jitter divisor table is unique to this malware family and enables reliable attribution across builds.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2043962204861423846

item.title: Phantom in the vault: Obsidian abused to deliver PhantomPulse RAT
item.description:
Novel campaign exploits Obsidian note-taking app to deliver PHANTOMPULSE RAT via weaponized community plugins. Cross-platform attack targets financial/crypto sectors through elaborate LinkedIn and Telegram social engineering.
Key technical details: • REF6598 campaign abuses Shell Commands and Hider plugins in shared Obsidian vaults for initial access • PHANTOMPULSE RAT features blockchain-based C2 resolution via Ethereum transaction data parsing from wallet 0xc117688c530b660e15085bF3A2B664117d8672aA • Windows chain: PHANTOMPULL loader uses AES-256-CBC decryption, timer queue callbacks, and reflective PE loading entirely in memory • macOS payload deploys obfuscated AppleScript with Telegram dead-drop fallback (t[.]me/ax03bot) • C2 weakness identified: anyone can hijack infrastructure by sending competing transactions to monitored wallet • IOCs: 195.3.222[.]251 staging server, panel.fefea22134[.]net C2 panel
Attack requires victim to manually enable community plugin sync after receiving shared vault credentials, then plugins auto-execute on vault open.
Hunt for child processes (PowerShell, bash) spawning from Obsidian with network connections. Monitor for obsidian-shellcommands directory creation and anomalous process execution from trusted applications. Full YARA rules and IOCs in report.

item.title: When deleting Signal is not enough: the FBI, iPhone notifications, and what forensics can reveal
item.description:
FBI recovers Signal messages from iPhone notification database after app deletion. No encryption broken—iOS preserved incoming message content in system files, demonstrating critical gap between user assumptions and data persistence.
Key technical details: • Messages recovered from /private/var/mobile/Library/BulletinBoard/ notification database • Only incoming messages accessible (went through Apple Push Notification service) • Signal's "notification preview" setting enabled, storing plaintext in iOS system storage • Data persists in AFU (After First Unlock) state even after app uninstallation • Likely extracted via logical acquisition or encrypted iTunes backup analysis
Attack surface: • iOS BulletinBoard framework maintains notification history for system continuity • Push notification tokens remain valid briefly after app removal • Commercial tools like Cellebrite UFED can access notification artifacts in AFU state • iCloud backups may also contain notification database contents when legally compelled
DFIR implications: • Notification databases now confirmed high-value artifact for iOS investigations • Check ClearedSections.plist for notification clearing evidence • Cross-reference with interactionC.db and sysdiagnose logs for timeline reconstruction • Consider notification content settings during mobile device analysis
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2042951348774183344

item.title: How Phishing Kits Targeting U.S. Giants Are Built, Sold, and Deployed
item.description:
Phishing-as-a-Service ecosystem targeting US 🇺🇸 corporations has matured into commodity cybercrime with $120 AiTM kits bypassing MFA. Tycoon 2FA alone sent 30M+ phishing emails monthly to 500K+ organizations by mid-2025.
Key technical details: • Adversary-in-the-Middle (AiTM) architecture captures authenticated session cookies post-MFA, not just credentials • Reverse proxy kits (Evilginx, EvilProxy) relay real-time traffic; application-driven models (Tycoon 2FA) clone login flows • Commercial pricing: Tycoon 2FA $120/10 days, EvilProxy $150-600/month, distributed via Telegram channels • Infrastructure abuse of trusted cloud providers: Cloudflare Pages incidents up 257% (460→1,370 cases 2023-2024) • Nation-state overlap: Russian 🇷🇺 Star Blizzard and Void Blizzard using same Evilginx tooling as cybercriminals
Attack chain: Phishing email → AiTM proxy/clone site → victim completes real MFA → session cookie harvested → account takeover. Scattered Spider leveraged this for $100M MGM Resorts breach.
Hunt for suspicious authentication patterns to trusted cloud domains and implement phishing-resistant FIDO2 rather than relying solely on traditional MFA.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2042695415225815176

item.title: Inside Predator’s kernel engine
item.description:
Commercial spyware Predator achieves full iOS kernel access through ARM NEON vector registers and defeats Apple's Pointer Authentication with a sophisticated JavaScriptCore gadget hunt. Analysis reveals previously undocumented kernel exploitation engine.
Technical deep dive: • FDGuardNeonRW primitive channels 528 bytes per read through NEON V0-V31 registers via thread_get_state/thread_set_state Mach APIs • PAC bypass hunts 20-byte PACIA X16,X17 gadget in JSC::JSArrayBuffer::isShared() within JavaScriptCore framework • 256-entry pre-computed PAC signing cache enables microsecond hook callbacks without cryptographic latency • RWTransfer mechanism shares kernel R/W between processes via guarded file descriptors and Mach port manipulation • callFunc framework hijacks thread state through Mach exception messages for remote function execution • Supports 21 iPhone models (XS through 14 Pro Max) across 5 device classes with precise kernel offset configurations
Key artifacts: Exception messages with msgh_id 2406, trojan threads at breakpoints, TaskROPDevOff.h source references, and uname() XOR comparisons for device fingerprinting.
Hunt for unsigned threads with continuous Mach exceptions and JavaScriptCore dlopen/dlsym patterns in suspicious processes.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2042679901828841849

item.title: Security’s Blind Spot: Physical Keyloggers That Bypass Antivirus Entirely
item.description:
NVISO Labs tested the AirDrive Forensic Keylogger Cable Pro, revealing how physical keyloggers create detection blind spots that completely bypass EDR systems. The device sits between keyboard and system as a USB HID passthrough, leaving zero OS-level artifacts.
Key findings: • Device creates WiFi hotspot (default SSID: AIR_XXYYZZ) for remote log access at 192[.]168[.]4[.]1 • USB power analysis shows distinctive current spikes (0.087A vs 0.007A baseline) during WiFi transmissions • Compatible only with standard office keyboards, not gaming keyboards with additional features • No processes, API calls, or network traces - operates as legitimate peripheral to OS • Investigation shifts from digital artifacts to physical access logs and camera footage
Detection approaches: • Rogue WiFi monitoring for suspicious SSIDs/BSSIDs in enterprise environments • USB power measurement shows periodic spikes but requires dedicated hardware • Physical inspections of cable runs, inline dongles behind monitors/docks • Asset management verification of keyboard models/serials vs records
Counter with strict physical access controls, FIDO2/WebAuthn passwordless MFA, tamper-evident seals on high-risk workstations, and continuous WLAN monitoring for unauthorized access points.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2042664813332189347

item.title: Fake Claude site installs malware that gives attackers access to your computer
item.description:
Fake Claude AI site distributes PlugX malware via convincing installer. Attackers exploit Claude's 290M monthly visits, deploying trojanized "Claude-Pro" that works normally while establishing persistent backdoor access.
Campaign mechanics: • Fake site hosts Claude-Pro-windows-x64.zip with MSI installer to misspelled path C:\Program Files (x86)\Anthropic\Claude\Cluade\ • VBScript dropper (Claude.vbs) launches legitimate Claude app while copying PlugX triad to Startup folder • DLL sideloading via legitimate signed G DATA updater NOVUpdate.exe + malicious avk.dll + encrypted NOVUpdate.exe.dat (T1574.002) • C2 callback to 8[.]217[.]190[.]58:443 within 22 seconds, hosted on Alibaba Cloud infrastructure • Anti-forensic cleanup: dropper self-deletes via ~del.vbs.bat, errors suppressed with "On Error Resume Next"
This reuses February 2026 Lab52-documented technique with fresh AI lure. Hunt for NOVUpdate.exe in Startup folders and outbound connections to defanged IP. Full IOC hashes available in report.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2042649597420384301

item.title: Dark Web Profile: TeamPCP
item.description:
TeamPCP executed 2026's most devastating supply chain attack, weaponizing security tools like Trivy and KICS to harvest 500,000 credentials from 1,000+ SaaS environments. The campaign cascaded across GitHub Actions, Docker, npm, PyPI, and OpenVSX ecosystems.
Key technical details: • Initial access via CVE-2026-33634 (CVSS 9.4) - incomplete token rotation after Feb 27 Pwn Request attack on Trivy CI/CD • Novel steganography: Python payloads embedded as base64 in WAV files with valid RIFF headers to evade detection • CanisterWorm: Self-propagating npm malware that auto-infected victim-maintained packages via stolen publish tokens • Imposter commits spoofed trusted contributor identities with backdated timestamps (T1036) • First documented abuse of Internet Computer Protocol (ICP) blockchain for C2: tdtqy-oyaaa-aaaae-af2dq-cai[.]raw[.]icp0[.]io
Attack methodology: • Credential harvesting via /proc/[pid]/mem scraping of GitHub Actions Runner.Worker processes • Fallback exfiltration: Created "tpcp-docs" repos in victim orgs, uploaded encrypted archives as release assets • Persistence: systemd services (pgmon.service) with Restart=always + privileged Kubernetes DaemonSets • Geopolitical targeting: Iran-specific wiper (rm -rf /) while deploying backdoors elsewhere
Major victims include European Commission 🇪🇺 AWS (92GB stolen), Aqua Security, Checkmarx, Palo Alto Networks, Telnyx, and 66+ npm packages. Campaign remains active with Vect Ransomware partnership announced.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2042634581925400686

item.title: Microsoft Recall Flaw Exposes Decrypted User Data, Researchers Find
item.description:
Microsoft Recall security flaw allows standard user processes to extract decrypted screenshots and OCR data through DLL injection into unprotected AIXHost.exe component. Microsoft closed case as "Not a Vulnerability" despite proof-of-concept.
Technical details: • AIXHost.exe (timeline renderer) lacks PPL protection unlike aihost.exe, creating trust boundary gap • TotalRecall tool uses CreateToolhelp32Snapshot/VirtualAllocEx APIs for DLL injection - no admin required • Extracts full PNG screenshots, OCR text with pixel coordinates, URLs, timestamps via COM interfaces • Some functions like GetRecentCaptureThumbnail bypass Windows Hello authentication entirely • DiscardDataAccess patching maintains persistent access after Recall closes
Attack chain: • User authenticates via Windows Hello (natural or Win+J trigger) • Decrypted data flows through vulnerable AIXHost.exe process • Standard DLL injection embeds totalrecall_payload.dll • COM interface abuse extracts 90-day retention database (ukg.db) • Stealth mode patches access controls for indefinite persistence
DFIR artifacts: • Monitor process injection into AIXHost.exe via Sysmon EID 8 • Unusual DLL loads in %ProgramFiles%\WindowsApps\MicrosoftCorporationII.WindowsSubsystemForAndroid • COM object access patterns to Recall interfaces without user interaction • Modifications to ukg.db outside normal Recall operations
Hunt for CreateToolhelp32Snapshot calls targeting AIXHost.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2042513772888142235

item.title: What to Know About CyberAv3ngers: The IRGC-Linked Group Targeting Critical Infrastructure
item.description:
Iranian 🇮🇷 state group CyberAv3ngers escalates from PLC defacement to deploying custom ICS malware and exploiting unpatchable Rockwell CVE-2021-22681 across US 🇺🇸 critical infrastructure. Six-agency advisory confirms operational disruption and financial losses.
Technical evolution shows deliberate capability escalation: • Phase 1-2 (2020-2024): Default credential exploitation on 75+ Unitronics PLCs • Phase 3 (2024): IOCONTROL malware targeting Linux IoT/OT devices via MQTT over TLS (port 8883) • Phase 4 (2026): Active exploitation of CVE-2021-22681 (CVSS 9.8) authentication bypass in Rockwell Logix controllers
Key DFIR artifacts: • Network traffic on ports 44818, 2222, 102, 22, 502 from overseas VPS providers • MQTT over TLS and DNS-over-HTTPS from OT segments • Unauthorized Studio 5000 Logix Designer connections to PLCs • Modified PLC project files and manipulated HMI/SCADA displays
Critical impact factors: • No vendor patch available for CVE-2021-22681 - only defense-in-depth mitigations • Techniques proliferated to 60+ affiliated groups creating "swarm effect" • Confirmed targeting: water/wastewater, energy, government sectors
Immediate action: Disconnect internet-facing PLCs, set physical mode switches to "Run", implement secure gateways with MFA. Full IOC list in CISA AA26-097A.
#DFIR_Radar | View on X: https://x.com/DFIR_Radar/status/2042347593112404002
